summary: Graham argues that the best startups behave like charities early on: focus on making something people truly want and defer monetization, because benevolence is a powerful competitive advantage. He connects this to real examples (Craigslist, Google, early Microsoft) and explains that being good boosts morale, attracts help, and provides a decisive compass for product choices.
key_points: The combination of user-focused product building and delayed monetization makes startups resemble charities, and that resemblance is a feature, not a bug.
Benevolence strengthens morale, keeps founders working through downturns, and makes startups harder to kill.
Being good attracts allies—investors, customers, and especially top talent who want to work on meaningful problems.
A user-first ethic is a stateless decision rule that simplifies hard choices and scales in complex environments.
money_quotes: Make something people want. Don't worry too much about making money. What you've got is a description of a charity.
You grow big by being nice, but you can stay big by being mean.
If you're benevolent, people will rally around you: investors, customers, other companies, and potential employees.
Here's the answer: Do whatever's best for your users. You can hold onto this like a rope in a hurricane, and it will save you if anything can.
Don't just not be evil. Be good.
key_insight: Treating “be good to users” as a strategic rule—rather than a moral slogan—creates the strongest startups.
